title: Google I/O 2025 – w_W2brDZDko
text:
[MUSIC PLAYING] RICHARD SEROTER: All
right, welcome, everybody. What's new in Cloud. Now, my name is Richard. I'm going to spend the next 45
minutes or so giving you about 1,000 announcements that I
expect you to all remember, by the end of this. It's going to be great. So ton happening in Cloud. Hopefully, you already got
excited by other keynotes today. So we're going to jump
right in and talk about, of course, what's new with AI. It's contractually obligated
for me as an employee at Google to start with AI. So let's do that. What's new with models? What should we be
excited about here? I'm going to try to put
these things in context, not just drown you
in announcements. But what's interesting
and new with models? Hey, look, if you're using
something like Vertex, you're now sitting here with
hundreds of curated foundation models, not just from Google,
from great open models, great partners. So you really do have an
amazing set of choices when you're using
Google Cloud to pick your favorite model of choice,
if that's somehow not Gemini. A lot of things in
preview, you saw today. Gemini 2.5 Pro is going
to be coming GA shortly. Gemini 2.5 Flash coming soon. The live API, some
amazing things you're seeing with
the ability to do different types of interactions,
live with the model. Veo 3, that was
amazing stuff I think we all saw, being
able to create video with audio and other things,
having that available on Vertex. Imagen 4 doing some
remarkable things with images, Lyra with music. And of course, now,
generally available, you have Veo 2 as well as Llama 4. We continue to bring
models like Llama 4. This is as a service. You do not deploy this
on infrastructure. You don't pay for
unused capacity. You're literally just calling
some of these open models, a number of them. Just pay for the tokens,
and that's all you pay for. So we're trying to make
it easier and easier to use our best models as well
as those from the community. Let's talk about Vertex AI. This is Google
Cloud's AI platform. This is how you build models.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – w_W2brDZDko
text:
Just pay for the tokens,
and that's all you pay for. So we're trying to make
it easier and easier to use our best models as well
as those from the community. Let's talk about Vertex AI. This is Google
Cloud's AI platform. This is how you build models. This is how you build agents. This is how you train. This is how you do all
sorts of interesting things. Let's talk about
what's new in Vertex. Are you familiar
with the optimizer? The optimizer is a
great way to make sure that you can send in a request
and actually rely on the service to pick the right
model for your request. Might pick Flash,
might pick Pro, but gives you some
different ways to let the service
actually decide what's the most cost effective thing? What's the fastest
thing for you? It's a really cool service. If you think about all
the pretraining options you have with an
LLM, we're trying to support all of these and
some of these in new ways. Of course, you can do things
like supervised fine tuning. This is where you can now use
this with things like Gemini 2. But you get your
own labeled data, and you want to take your data
and make the model better, more contextual to you. Of course, you can do
retrieval-augmented generation, and we support this
now with more models of how do you use external
data to complement your model and give it more
contextual responses? You can do full fine tuning now
with models, as you see here, some new ones supported,
which is terrific, as well as in-context learning. This is being able to just pass
in more things to long context. Gemini continues to
have some of the best long context in the industry. So shove a bunch
of data in there and get back really
contextual responses. So making it easier and easier
to make these models for you. How about the Vertex
AI global endpoint? This is a really
nice way if you're not location sensitive to say,
just put global in the URL, and don't pick US
Central 1 or US West 3. You don't care about that. Just give me the most available
performant work for my AI model,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – w_W2brDZDko
text:
This is a really
nice way if you're not location sensitive to say,
just put global in the URL, and don't pick US
Central 1 or US West 3. You don't care about that. Just give me the most available
performant work for my AI model, which is terrific. This doesn't work
with everything if you do context caching and
stuff, so read the fine print. But this gives you
a really nice way to just say I'm not location
sensitive for this workload. Just make it fast and easy. Agent Builder, how
do you build agents? We're all agent builders now,
whether you want to be or not. It's exciting. So if you look at
the family of things that we do now with agents,
you might be developing agents. Of course, we're at a
developer conference. How do I use some of the
different tools, the agent development kit, which
we'll be talking about. How do I do things in low
code, use the agent garden and use existing
agents, or even see how you can integrate
with other tools? How do you connect
agents together? You've seen us embracing the
model context protocol, MCP. How do these agents
talk to tools? How do they pull in
really interesting things from outside of the system. Even the agent-to-agent
protocol and others, how do you deploy
this and run this? We now have Vertex AI Agent
Engine, which we'll talk about. Run it on GKE, Cloud Run,
all sorts of great places. And of course, day two matters. How do I do troubleshooting? How do I figure
out observability? All of this thing is a family
of things we call the Vertex AI Agent Builder, and you can mix
and match however you'd like. Let's talk about the ADK. It's a very, very
exciting project. We just hit 1.0 on Python
today, which is exciting. But I'm actually
really stoked that we shipped the first
version of the Java ADK. So if you were a
Java developer-- you got Java
developers in the room? It's OK to admit it. There we go. [APPLAUSE] That's right. No shame. If you love Java,
now you can actually build great Agentic
applications. This is a really
sophisticated framework
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – w_W2brDZDko
text:
you got Java
developers in the room? It's OK to admit it. There we go. [APPLAUSE] That's right. No shame. If you love Java,
now you can actually build great Agentic
applications. This is a really
sophisticated framework where you can do simple things
or hard things, use session, do complicated
multi-agent things. Really, really awesome. Docs are terrific. I know the people
who wrote them. You should use
them and read them. So exciting stuff
if you are trying to use an open framework from
Google, where you can take that agent and run it anywhere. As you see the agent
development kit, and we'll see a demo shortly. But really, if you just
have instructions, a model, some tools, some of
these components, you can really pull together an
agent that involves no coding. You're doing very sophisticated
things with honestly no code. What do I want to
have the outcome be? What tools do you
have available to you? And which model should I use? Plug in non-Gemini models. That's fine. Call tools that are
outside of Google Cloud. Live your life. It's fine. It all works great. So this makes it really easy
to build modern agents however you'd like to do it. And so use the Agent
Development Kit to build these sort of
agents, test it locally, which is really powerful, a really
sophisticated tool there, which we'll see in a second. Pick your models. You want to use
something from Anthropic. You want to use
something from Meta. That's great. Go for it. Now, if you don't
want to do Python, nobody's going to judge you. Use Java. It's amazing. Pick that as well,
whatever works for you. And then run this and
connect to different services and different clouds thanks to
this great tool support and MCP support. If you got to pull data from
some other cloud's object storage, it's OK. Do that. So one of the big things to
take away here is look at ADK. Look at ways that you can--
all of us can build agents. Now, it's great
that you have MCP for how does an agent
actually pull data from tools. Very important. Everyone in the world
is now using it.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – w_W2brDZDko
text:
So one of the big things to
take away here is look at ADK. Look at ways that you can--
all of us can build agents. Now, it's great
that you have MCP for how does an agent
actually pull data from tools. Very important. Everyone in the world
is now using it. But how do the agents
talk to each other? How do they share each
other's capabilities or share state between each other? That's why Google created the
agent-to-agent protocol along with a bunch of other companies. Microsoft adopted this last
week, which is exciting. You're seeing more and
more companies say, this is a really powerful
way to have agents talk to each other, good protocol. And now we have an
SDK for this in Python that we shipped today as well. So you have an easier
way to build these. So as you think
of MCP for tools, A2A is how agents
can communicate. Agent Engine, where do
you run these darn things? Well, you have a lot of choices. And so the Vertex AI
Agent Engine gives you a fully managed service
that can take your agent, run it, scale it, handle all
the operational concerns, give you some observability,
gives you session management. Really simple to use. Just today we lit up
the new dashboard. So it has a new
dashboard if you're somebody like me who doesn't
love using CLIs all the time. So it's great. So you have a new
visual interface, great way to run
services available today. And Agent Engine, again,
is part of this ecosystem. There is no one thing
that builds agents. Agents are part of
how do you build this? You might use
something like ADK. You might run this in
something like Agent Engine. You might register it in
something like Agent Space. You might access data in
things like our databases or other tools or even
Google Workspace services. So there's a whole ecosystem
of things that come together when you're building agents. There's not just one tool. So I think this is a
great way to represent it. With that, I just can't show
you slides the whole time. I'd like to invite my friend
Fran on stage to actually give you a live demo.

with an API key. But I decided I'm not going
to share my key today. RICHARD SEROTER: No? Low trust environment here? OK. FRAN HINKELMANN: So I'm using
the typical Google Cloud setup that you use to specify your
project and your location. OK. OK. So the agent I'm showing

few other things here. And then Richard was saying,

was complicated. [LAUGHTER] FRAN HINKELMANN: So Agent

it to not hallucinate because if we're doing
data science science, please no hallucinations.

some data science Pandas code that the agent is

That's an agent, right? What you're showing us, that's

here, the agent is not making up the graph. The agent is creating the code
that then deterministically

And I give you data, and I

then it's probably right. And it's fully
relying on the data. This is not a made up LLM

You don't like blue? RICHARD SEROTER:

I like Cloud Run. GKE is a good option. And of course, Vertex Agent
Engine is a good spot for agent. Agent Engine is a
fully-managed auto scaling service on Google Cloud
that's specifically designed for agents. So I suggest go build an agent
that makes your life easier, and deploy it to the Cloud. Back to you, Richard. RICHARD SEROTER: Thanks, Fran. Great job. [APPLAUSE] Yeah, and those SDK

Engine and things like that. So we have made
that pretty easy. All right, we're not
done with agents. No, we are not. Let's talk about Agent
Space a little bit. So when you think about how
do I-- what's the right way to take some of these agents and
put these interfaces in there? It's not going to be that
web interface, most likely. How do you serve
up agents to users? How do you make them
available to folks? Agent Space is for your
employees, your people to get advantage, take
the advantage of Google quality search, agents that
connect to employee directories, third-party systems,
Box, Dropbox, all these sorts of systems,
databases, your email, others. How does this make it easier
to pull all that information in and actually make things
like an intranet feel useful? And so this is almost like an
enterprise operating system. Your home page, how
do I work better? In your environment, Agent Space
can take third-party agents, can take agents you build, can
take out-of-the-box agents. Really, really neat way to
serve this up to your employees and make this something
they can interact with.

In your environment, Agent Space

on some of these things in some of the
announcements today. But three things I
wanted to point out. First, there's that
Gemma 3n model. This gives you a remarkably
small, high-performing model that you can run
on mobile devices. Again, you could run

for the LiteRT Hugging Face community as you have more
models you can take advantage of on your mobile devices. And then finally,
even being able to do on-device retrieval-augmented
generation, bringing more functionality to
what you can do locally on a mobile device is
pretty exciting stuff. So stay tuned to this space. A lot of innovations
happening in small places. What's new for app developers? This is the part I get even more
excited about, as a lot of us are at least pretend
developers or real developers. What are we excited about? What's new here in Cloud for us? Gemini Code Assist is our
flagship coding assistance tool that you can use
with Google Cloud and get code
completion, generation, training on your own code base,

it really straightforward to pull in some really, really
popular systems into your IDE

I don't have to
remember that that's one of the things I want to do. Put systems instructions in
custom rules and the config, much easier to prompt. And the last thing,

I spend quite a bit of time on refining my prompts. And a lot of times,
I want to go back to something I did a week
ago or see the response. But I restarted my
machine on VS Code, and all these super smart
answers and really good prompts that I came
up with are gone. Code Assist, if you go up here
to the little History icon, you get a history of all your
chats, even if you restart. So now I can go
back and be like, what was I doing last week? What was the answer? How did I write that prompt? And I can see all my prompts. I love it, I think. I think that's something
you need to know if you use Gemini Code Assist. RICHARD SEROTER: Yeah, love it. Thank you so much, Fran. FRAN HINKELMANN: Thank you. RICHARD SEROTER: Wonderful. [APPLAUSE] Yes, you can use this
today in the enterprise with a subscription. You can use it free
for individuals today with remarkably
high usage limits, that unless you're a
robot, you'll never hit. So take advantage of
it, and use it for fun. Let's talk about a few more
things for app developers. How do you design architectures? How do you design systems? Sometimes the Cloud can feel
like a pile of infrastructure. It's a user role. It's a storage bucket. It's a bunch of
Kubernetes services, cloud run services, databases. It's hard to organize it all. So Application
Design Center is part of the set of tools we've
built that actually makes it possible to visually model
a system, deploy that system, and then even be able to operate
